topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom was a glittering expanse of forced merriment, the kind where everyone was smiling a little too brightly. I nursed a lukewarm champagne flute, the bubbles doing little to settle the hollow ache in my chest. Every conversation felt like a performance I wasn't starring in. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. Liam. He was laughing, his head tilted back, the light catching the familiar curve of his jaw. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the ambient noise. He turned, and for a split second, the music seemed to fade, leaving only the echo of a shared past. His eyes swept the room, and for a fleeting moment, they landed on mine. A wave of something heavy and stagnant washed over me, making my hands grip the glass tighter. I managed a weak smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and quickly looked down at my shoes, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I stood near the periphery of the receiving line, observing the carefully curated joy of the wedding guests. The sheer volume of happy chatter felt like a physical pressure building behind my ribs. I kept my gaze fixed on the floral arrangements, cataloging the shades of pink and ivory, anything to avoid making eye contact with anyone. It was suffocating, this forced togetherness. Then, I saw him. Standing near the entrance, talking animatedly with someone, was Alex. The air seemed to thin out around me. My feet felt rooted to the marble floor. When he glanced over, a flicker of recognition—a ghost of a memory—crossed his face before he masked it with a polite nod. A strange, cold tightening began in my stomach. I forced myself to take a slow sip of water, the coolness doing little to soothe the internal tremor. I wanted to dissolve into the wallpaper, to simply cease existing in this vibrant, overwhelming space.

[story 3]

The background noise of the celebration was a constant, irritating hum. I found myself leaning against a pillar, pretending to examine a tapestry, trying to make myself small. The sheer proximity of so many people, all connected by shared history and present happiness, made me feel profoundly detached. It was a heavy cloak draped over my shoulders. Then, I spotted him near the buffet table, dressed impeccably, looking utterly at ease amidst the chaos. It was Sarah. Her presence instantly disrupted the monotonous drone of the evening. When our eyes met across the room, time seemed to stutter. A deep, unfamiliar yearning pulsed through me, a painful reminder of what was lost. I felt a sudden, desperate urge to flee, to simply walk out the nearest exit and disappear into the anonymity of the night, leaving this suffocating warmth behind.
